## 

## **Remote Solenoid Load Controller using Particle Photon 2 and Web Interface**

## **Course: EE 1301 – Introduction to IoT Systems (Final Project)**

## **Student: Adam Hang([hang0162@umn.edu](mailto:hang0162@umn.edu))**

		**Alex LIu(qinch002@umn.edu)**

## **Date: December 9, 2025**

## **Project Description**

\-	Problem Addressed

The Solenoid Load Controller project addresses the need for precise remote control of an electromagnetic solenoid’s force output. As in for the first attempt we used uln 2003 as a form of driver in order to control the solenoid. However, we are looking for precision in distance control. Hence, just by accepting digital signals of on and off for uln2003 is far from our goal. Instead we use another approach. By using a N-type MOSFET, we are able to use photon 2 platform to control the voltage input of the solenoid. Through this method, we are able to use Analogwrite() to precise the amount of voltage going towards the solenoid.

## **Background**

A solenoid is an electromechanical device that converts electrical energy into linear motion. It consists of a coil of wire which, when energized with current, produces a magnetic field that pulls a metal plunger or core. In many basic IoT projects, solenoids (or relays that contain solenoids) are used as binary devices – fully energized or completely off . However, solenoids can also be driven in an analog manner. In a proportional solenoid configuration, the force exerted by the plunger is roughly proportional to the coil current over a range of travel . By controlling the current (via voltage) continuously, one can achieve incremental positioning or force output rather than just two states. For example, increasing the drive current will increase the magnetic force and pull the plunger further against a spring, reaching a new equilibrium position . This principle is used in proportional valves and other actuators where a variable output is needed.

**Driving a solenoid:** To control a solenoid from a microcontroller, a driver circuit is required because solenoids are inductive loads that typically draw more current (and often higher voltage) than a microcontroller can supply directly. A common driver is a power transistor or MOSFET that switches the solenoid’s current on command from a microcontroller. In our design, a logic-level N-channel MOSFET was used to connect and disconnect the solenoid from the supply under PWM/DAC control. A critical component in the driver is the flyback diode placed across the solenoid coil (in reverse bias to the supply). This diode safely shunts the high-voltage spike that occurs when the solenoid is de-energized (the collapsing magnetic field induces a voltage spike) . Without this diode, the voltage spike could damage the transistor or the microcontroller. We used a 1N4007 diode for this purpose, providing a path for the induced current and thus protecting the rest of the circuit.

**Particle Photon 2 and DAC:** The Particle Photon 2 board was chosen as the controller for its IoT capabilities – it has built-in Wi-Fi and integration with Particle’s cloud. Importantly, unlike many microcontrollers, the Photon 2 supports analog output either through a built-in DAC pin or high-resolution PWM. In our project, we integrate a DAC module to generate a smooth analog voltage to drive the solenoid. A true analog output (as opposed to a rapidly switched PWM) helps avoid any high-frequency noise in the solenoid’s operation, resulting in more stable and linear force control. The DAC (with a 0–5 V output range) feeds into the gate of the MOSFET or an amplifier stage that then drives the 12 V solenoid coil. This allows the control system to regulate the solenoid’s coil voltage from 0 up to the maximum, thereby controlling current and magnetic force continuously.

## **Related Work**

Internet-connected actuators are a well-explored area in IoT, and our project builds on ideas from prior works. For example, smart irrigation systems often use IoT controllers to actuate solenoid water valves remotely. One community project on the Particle forum aimed to switch a water solenoid valve via smartphone, using a Particle Photon and a battery supply . That project was primarily on/off control, but it demonstrated the feasibility of using Particle devices for wireless solenoid actuation. Our work extends this idea by adding analog control for variable flow or force.

Another relevant project example is the “waterfall printer” installation discussed in the Particle community, where a user controlled 40 solenoids with a Photon to create patterns in falling water . To manage so many solenoids, they explored driver circuits (transistor arrays, MOSFETs) and addressed issues of power distribution and speed. This relates to our project in highlighting the hardware challenges of solenoid control (transient suppression, current requirements, expansion via driver ICs) and the role of Particle’s I/O limits. While our project controls a single solenoid (or a few) with analog levels, lessons from that large-scale project – such as using MOSFETs for fast switching and ensuring a proper power supply – informed our design.

In terms of cloud integration, Particle’s platform is known for enabling web-based device control. Numerous tutorials (e.g., using Particle to drive relays) show how everyday devices can be controlled over the internet using the Particle Cloud’s functions and API . We followed a similar approach by creating cloud functions on the Photon 2 that our web interface calls to adjust the solenoid output. This project thus sits at the intersection of existing ideas: it combines IoT remote control (commonly implemented in smart home projects) with analog actuator control (found in industrial automation). To our knowledge, few hobbyist projects have demonstrated a Particle-based solenoid driver with variable output, making our work a novel contribution in the IoT project space.

## **Solution Overview**

OUr solution is to have a combination of web page control in align with 

**System functionality:** In practice, once the system is powered (e.g., a 12 V supply for the solenoid and a USB or regulator for the Photon), the user can open the control webpage from anywhere. The page might show a slider labeled “Solenoid Force” and perhaps a real-time readout of the level. If the user moves the slider, a cloud function call is triggered behind the scenes. Within a second or two (depending on internet latency), the Photon receives the new value and updates the DAC output. The solenoid’s magnetic field then increases or decreases to match the command. We observed this by feeling the pull on the solenoid’s plunger and by measuring the coil current with a multimeter. The system also includes safety timeouts: for example, if no command is received for a long period, the firmware can revert the solenoid to an off state to prevent overheating. Overall, the **solution design** effectively demonstrates remote analog control – the user can modulate a physical actuator from a web browser in real time, with the Particle cloud seamlessly handling the communication.

**Component:**

\-	Particle Photon 2 –For processing various programs and signals

	N-type MOSFET(for solenoid)

\-	BM-0530B solenoid(5N max)

\-	220 ohms resistor: circuit protection

\-	ULN2003 stepper driver

\-	1N4007 Diode

\-           MPC4821: The 12-bit DAC processes various signals and analog inputs, sending an initial voltage to the high-voltage amplifier.

\-	LM2904: Comparator: When high-voltage op-amp chips are unavailable, it boosts the limited 3.3V voltage to 5.0V while simultaneously applying a bias voltage to the sample stage.

\-	Buzzer: Divided into four quadrants, responsible for moving the probe at the nanoscale level along the x, y, and z axes.

**Code Design**

Code(for solenoid):

void setup() {

   Serial.begin(115200);

   pinMode(SOLENOID\_PIN, OUTPUT);

   analogWrite(SOLENOID\_PIN, 0);

   pinMode(DAC\_CS, OUTPUT);

   digitalWrite(DAC\_CS, HIGH);

   SPI.begin();

   Particle.variable("UserSetLoad", UserSetLoad);

   Particle.function("ResetLoad", ResetLoad);

   Particle.function("SetLoadLevel", SetLoadLevel);

   Particle.function("setParams", setParams);

}

void applyLoadLevel() {

   int duty \= 0;

   switch (UserSetLoad) {

       case 1: duty \= 64;  break;

       case 2: duty \= 128; break;

       case 3: duty \= 192; break;

       case 4: duty \= 255; break;

       default:

           UserSetLoad \= 1;

           duty \= 64;

           break;

   }

   analogWrite(SOLENOID\_PIN, duty);

}

int ResetLoad(String arg) {

   UserSetLoad \= 1;

   applyLoadLevel();

   analogWrite(SOLENOID\_PIN, 0);

   resetPulseActive \= true;

   resetStartTime \= millis();

   return 1;

}

For the primary approaching system , we utilize analogWrite() function which through a N type MOSFET directly controls the amount of voltage directed toward the solenoid. Meanwhile, for the cloud html control system. We utilize the method of declaring cloud variables and functions and link them toward html access. Through these connections ,we are able to build consistent circulation. Now on the solenoid side of code. The solenoid we bought have a maximum fore exertion of 5 Newtons and 10 mm of distance. To have much more precision, we separate the voltage level(solenoid force) into 4 levels. Each level corresponds to a certain amount of voltage allowed through the N type MOSFET. Level1(25%)-\> analogWrite(solenoidPin,64), Level2(50%) analogWrite(solenoidPin,127.5), and finally Level3(75%)analogWrite(solenoidPin,192), and same way for 100%. Furthermore than that we also have a function fo resetting the solenoid to default of level1 just for the sake of the aging of solenoid and prevent overheat of the inductor inside the solenoid

Code for combination(Motor and circuit control)

**\#include "Particle.h"**

**// \--------------------------------------**

**// CONFIG**

**// \--------------------------------------**

**const int SOLENOID\_PIN \= D1;   // Your output pin**

**const int DAC\_CS \= D10;        // DAC chip select (from your previous code)**

**int UserSetLoad \= 1;           // Load level 1–4**

**float currentBias \= 0;**

**int scanStep \= 100;**

**bool resetPulseActive \= false;**

**unsigned long resetStartTime \= 0;**

**const unsigned long RESET\_PULSE\_MS \= 200;**

**// \--------------------------------------**

**// FUNCTION DECLARATIONS**

**// \--------------------------------------**

**int ResetLoad(String arg);**

**int SetLoadLevel(String arg);**

**int setParams(String args);**

**void applyLoadLevel();**

**void sendToDAC(uint16\_t value);**

**uint16\_t voltageToDAC(float v);**

**// \--------------------------------------**

**// SETUP**

**// \--------------------------------------**

**void setup() {**

    **Serial.begin(115200);**

    **pinMode(SOLENOID\_PIN, OUTPUT);**

    **analogWrite(SOLENOID\_PIN, 0);**

    **pinMode(DAC\_CS, OUTPUT);**

    **digitalWrite(DAC\_CS, HIGH);**

    **SPI.begin();**

    **Particle.variable("UserSetLoad", UserSetLoad);**

    **Particle.function("ResetLoad", ResetLoad);**

    **Particle.function("SetLoadLevel", SetLoadLevel);**

    **Particle.function("setParams", setParams);**

**}**

**// \--------------------------------------**

**// DAC SUPPORT**

**// \--------------------------------------**

**uint16\_t voltageToDAC(float v) {**

    **if (v \< 0\) v \= 0;**

    **if (v \> 3.3) v \= 3.3;**

    **return (uint16\_t)((v / 3.3) \* 4095);**

**}**

**void sendToDAC(uint16\_t val) {**

    **val &= 0x0FFF;**

    **uint16\_t packet \= 0b0011000000000000 | val;**

    **uint8\_t hi \= (packet \>\> 8\) & 0xFF;**

    **uint8\_t lo \= packet & 0xFF;**

    **digitalWrite(DAC\_CS, LOW);**

    **SPI.transfer(hi);**

    **SPI.transfer(lo);**

    **digitalWrite(DAC\_CS, HIGH);**

**}**

**// \--------------------------------------**

**// SAMPLE BIAS FUNCTION (setParams)**

**// \--------------------------------------**

**int setParams(String args) {**

    **// Parse JSON manually**

    **int biasIndex \= args.indexOf("sample\_bias\_V");**

    **int stepIndex \= args.indexOf("scan\_step");**

    **if (biasIndex \< 0\) return \-1;**

    **if (stepIndex \< 0\) return \-2;**

    **// Extract numbers**

    **int biasPos \= args.indexOf(":", biasIndex);**

    **int biasEnd \= args.indexOf(",", biasPos);**

    **currentBias \= args.substring(biasPos \+ 1, biasEnd).toFloat();**

    **int stepPos \= args.indexOf(":", stepIndex);**

    **int stepEnd \= args.indexOf("}", stepPos);**

    **scanStep \= args.substring(stepPos \+ 1, stepEnd).toInt();**

    **// Apply to DAC**

    **sendToDAC(voltageToDAC(currentBias));**

    **return 1;**

**}**

**// \--------------------------------------**

**// LOAD CONTROL**

**// \--------------------------------------**

**void applyLoadLevel() {**

    **int duty \= 0;**

    **switch (UserSetLoad) {**

        **case 1: duty \= 64;  break;**

        **case 2: duty \= 128; break;**

        **case 3: duty \= 192; break;**

        **case 4: duty \= 255; break;**

        **default:**

            **UserSetLoad \= 1;**

            **duty \= 64;**

            **break;**

    **}**

    **analogWrite(SOLENOID\_PIN, duty);**

**}**

**int SetLoadLevel(String arg) {**

    **int level \= arg.toInt();**

    **if (level \< 1 || level \> 4\) return \-1;**

    **UserSetLoad \= level;**

    **applyLoadLevel();**

    **return UserSetLoad;**

**}**

**int ResetLoad(String arg) {**

    **UserSetLoad \= 1;**

    **applyLoadLevel();**

    **analogWrite(SOLENOID\_PIN, 0);**

    **resetPulseActive \= true;**

    **resetStartTime \= millis();**

    **return 1;**

**}**

**// \--------------------------------------**

**// LOOP**

**// \--------------------------------------**

**void loop() {**

    **if (resetPulseActive) {**

        **if (millis() \- resetStartTime \>= RESET\_PULSE\_MS) {**

            **digitalWrite(SOLENOID\_PIN, LOW);**

            **resetPulseActive \= false;**

        **}**

    **}**

    **applyLoadLevel();**

**}**

In the final version of the code, we combined the motor control code with the circuit control code for easier management. We primarily employed two sets of function logic to independently control the stepper motor and the terminal control circuit of the STM (including applying bias voltage and manipulating the number of steps for the piezoelectric scanner). For stepper motor control, we opted to output digital signals to achieve relatively precise control. We configured the stepper motor to allow selection of up to four steps to enhance controllability, aiding in debugging the bias circuit and piezoelectric scanner. For the STM terminal control circuit, we have the MPC4821 EP output an initial voltage signal. This signal is then further amplified at its output pin (pin 8), which connects directly to the LM2904N acting as a comparator and simple boost converter. The amplified voltage is finally output to the piezoelectric scanner interface. Within the program, bias application serves as an additional function: it primarily outputs through pin 7 of the LM2904N. Consequently, we have established a series of logic to facilitate this functionality, which is integrated alongside the piezo scanner control code.

**Results and Reflection**

In this project, we achieved nanometer-level displacement in a single quadrant of the piezoelectric scanning head, a simplified implementation of sample stage bias application, and an innovative design for the coarse/fine approach mechanism. Future iterations should incorporate feedback loops, continuously optimize electronic material usage, integrate additional vibration damping systems, and account for factors like thermal expansion and noise affecting scanning performance. I believe this project tangibly resolves several challenges: for instance, our specialized design drastically reduces the mechanical components required for the STM, scaling back from the dozens typically found in conventional STMs to just three. This significantly enhances longevity, lowers maintenance costs, and improves repair efficiency. Over the next four years, we will continue refining the STM until we achieve our original vision.

**Electrical Schematics**

For the wiring of the solenoid, we apply a external 12V DC power supply, the \+12V linked to one wire of the solenoid and through the other wire, it connects to the drain of the N type MOSFET, in parallel with that is a 1N4007 diode which helps with the solenoid when the inductor encounter a sudden change in voltage when turning on and off the power source. The ground is link toward D1 on photon2 through a 220 Ohms resistor. The source is then connected to the ground on the photon which is also where the external 12V power source is connected to an N-type MOSFET and then to Photon2.

![][image1]

![][image2]

In the final version of the code, we combined the motor control code with the circuit control code for easier management. We primarily employed two sets of function logic to independently control the stepper motor and the terminal control circuit of the STM (including applying bias voltage and manipulating the number of steps for the piezoelectric scanner). For stepper motor control, we opted to output digital signals to achieve relatively precise control. We configured the stepper motor to allow selection of up to four steps to enhance controllability, aiding in debugging the bias circuit and piezoelectric scanner. For the STM terminal control circuit, we have the MPC4821 EP output an initial voltage signal. This signal is then further amplified at its output pin (pin 8), which connects directly to the LM2904N acting as a comparator and simple boost converter. The amplified voltage is finally output to the piezoelectric scanner interface. Within the program, bias voltage application serves as an additional function: it primarily outputs through pin 7 of the LM2904N. Consequently, we established a series of logic to facilitate this functionality, which is integrated alongside the piezoelectric scanner control code. The bias voltage application device uses a separate wire connected to the copper metal sample stage, while the STM enclosure serves as the ground line to ensure proper operation of circuits on the STM's mechanical components. Other pins are primarily used to connect to the Photon 2's GND or 3.3V interfaces, providing sufficient power to drive these chips. Additionally, we installed 150-ohm resistors and an NPN transistor at each output interface to protect the circuit against uncontrolled short circuits or electrical issues.

To verify circuit reliability, we wrote and tested validation code. First, for the MPC4821EP, to verify the functionality of its output pin (pin 8), we employed a program that periodically outputs 0V, 1V, 2V, 3V, 4V, and 5V. This program generates a regular trapezoidal waveform. We connected the oscilloscope probe to pin 8 and the ground clip to GND to test the DAC's operation. As demonstrated in the video provided below, the DAC passed our tests flawlessly outputting a perfect, typical trapezoidal waveform:

![][image3]

We also conducted tests on pin 7 of the LM2904. After assembling this section of the circuit, we wrote a program to generate a regular sawtooth wave—which is precisely the key to controlling the STM piezoelectric scanning head. Based on the test shown below, it appears to be quite successful. Due to the chip and inherent noise, the waveform displayed on the oscilloscope may not be perfectly clean and may contain some noise, but its shape and data are correct：![][image4]

**Code Listing**

\#include "Particle.h"

const int SOLENOID\_PIN \= D1;   // Your output pin

const int DAC\_CS \= D10;        // DAC chip select (from your previous code)

int UserSetLoad \= 1;           // Load level 1–4

float currentBias \= 0;

int scanStep \= 100;

bool resetPulseActive \= false;

unsigned long resetStartTime \= 0;

const unsigned long RESET\_PULSE\_MS \= 200;

**Annotation**

* \#include "Particle.h" brings in the Particle Device OS APIs (cloud functions, pins, SPI, etc.).

* SOLENOID\_PIN is the Photon pin that drives the solenoid using PWM (analogWrite).

* DAC\_CS is the chip-select line for the external DAC on the SPI bus.

* UserSetLoad is a cloud-controlled load level (1–4) that determines the PWM duty cycle applied to the solenoid.

* currentBias stores the bias voltage (in volts) to be output to the DAC.

* scanStep stores an integer step size used in the scan configuration (coming from the JSON string).

* resetPulseActive, resetStartTime, and RESET\_PULSE\_MS implement a **non-blocking timed reset pulse** for the solenoid using millis() instead of delay().

int ResetLoad(String arg);

int SetLoadLevel(String arg);

int setParams(String args);

void applyLoadLevel();

void sendToDAC(uint16\_t value);

uint16\_t voltageToDAC(float v);

**Annotation**

* The three int functions are **cloud-callable handlers**:

  * ResetLoad resets the solenoid position and load level.

  * SetLoadLevel changes the load level based on a string argument ("1"–"4").

  * setParams parses a JSON-like string to configure bias voltage and scan step.

* applyLoadLevel updates the PWM duty cycle based on UserSetLoad.

* sendToDAC and voltageToDAC convert a voltage into a 12-bit DAC code and send it out via SPI.

void setup() {

    Serial.begin(115200);

    pinMode(SOLENOID\_PIN, OUTPUT);

    analogWrite(SOLENOID\_PIN, 0);

    pinMode(DAC\_CS, OUTPUT);

    digitalWrite(DAC\_CS, HIGH);

    SPI.begin();

    Particle.variable("UserSetLoad", UserSetLoad);

    Particle.function("ResetLoad", ResetLoad);

    Particle.function("SetLoadLevel", SetLoadLevel);

    Particle.function("setParams", setParams);

}

**Annotation**

* Serial.begin(115200) enables serial debugging over USB.

* SOLENOID\_PIN is set as a digital output, and its PWM output is initialized to 0 (solenoid off).

* DAC\_CS is configured as an output and set HIGH, which deselects the DAC by default.

* SPI.begin() initializes the SPI bus for communication with the DAC.

* Particle.variable("UserSetLoad", UserSetLoad) exposes the load level as a **cloud-readable variable**, so a webpage or app can read it.

* Particle.function(...) binds three **cloud functions** so they can be called remotely by name:

  * "ResetLoad" → ResetLoad

  * "SetLoadLevel" → SetLoadLevel

  * "setParams" → setParams

uint16\_t voltageToDAC(float v) {

    if (v \< 0\) v \= 0;

    if (v \> 3.3) v \= 3.3;

    return (uint16\_t)((v / 3.3) \* 4095);

}

void sendToDAC(uint16\_t val) {

    val &= 0x0FFF;

    uint16\_t packet \= 0b0011000000000000 | val;

    uint8\_t hi \= (packet \>\> 8\) & 0xFF;

    uint8\_t lo \= packet & 0xFF;

    digitalWrite(DAC\_CS, LOW);

    SPI.transfer(hi);

    SPI.transfer(lo);

    digitalWrite(DAC\_CS, HIGH);

}

**Annotation**

* voltageToDAC:

  * Clamps the requested voltage into the valid DAC range (0–3.3 V).

  * Scales this voltage into a 12-bit digital code (0–4095) using a simple linear mapping.

* sendToDAC:

  * Masks the value to 12 bits (val &= 0x0FFF).

  * Builds a 16-bit command word (packet) that includes configuration bits plus the 12-bit data.

  * Splits the 16-bit word into two bytes (hi, lo) and sends them over SPI.

  * Toggles DAC\_CS low→high around the transfers to frame the write operation.

int ResetLoad(String arg) {

    UserSetLoad \= 1;

    applyLoadLevel();

    analogWrite(SOLENOID\_PIN, 0);

    resetPulseActive \= true;

    resetStartTime \= millis();

    return 1;

}

**Annotation**

* ResetLoad is used to reset the system from the cloud/UI.

* It:

  * Sets UserSetLoad back to 1 and reapplies the default load.

  * Instantly turns the solenoid PWM off (analogWrite(..., 0)).

  * Starts a timed “reset pulse window” by setting resetPulseActive and recording resetStartTime.

* The actual timing logic is handled later in loop() using millis().

void loop() {

    if (resetPulseActive) {

        if (millis() \- resetStartTime \>= RESET\_PULSE\_MS) {

            digitalWrite(SOLENOID\_PIN, LOW);

            resetPulseActive \= false;

        }

    }

    applyLoadLevel();

}

**Annotation**

* The loop continuously checks whether a reset pulse is active.

* If resetPulseActive is true, it compares the current time (millis()) with resetStartTime.

* Once the elapsed time exceeds RESET\_PULSE\_MS (200 ms), it:

  * Forces the solenoid pin fully low, ensuring it is de-energized.

  * Clears the resetPulseActive flag so the timer stops.

* applyLoadLevel() is called every loop iteration to keep the PWM output consistent with the latest UserSetLoad.

   This makes sure that if the cloud changes the load level while running, the solenoid follows it.

**AI Documentation**

We utilize AI to help us write the html file for connecting with the C++ code. Furthermore on that, we also ask AI for instructions on each electrical component on the circuit including diode, uln2003 driver, MPC4821, and LM2904,

Prompt listed below:

What are some of MOSFET module that would apply on the solenoid BM-0530B

help me create a html code that applies on Photon 2 board, using cloud variables and functions linked with a specific board, the html must have 2 buttons which linked 2 functions in the C++ code.1, "reset stepper motor".2, "reset solenoid".The program must ask user for an input and upload the input into the cloud variable and return a value. I just need the structure
